name: weakerpowers
description: A beginner-friendly, guided build-a-project workflow for people brand new to coding.
skillInstructions:
Kimi Code tool mapping for Weakerpowers skills:

- When a Weakerpowers skill says to ask the person something, ask one question at a time, present choices, or wait for their okay, call Kimi Code's `AskUserQuestion` tool. Keep the wording plain and jargon-free. Don't render choices as plain assistant text unless `AskUserQuestion` is unavailable or the session is in auto permission mode.
- For `AskUserQuestion`, ask 1 question with 2-4 concrete, plain-language options when possible. Put the recommended option first and suffix its label with `(Recommended)`.
- When a Weakerpowers skill refers to `TodoWrite`, use Kimi Code's `TodoList` tool. If a skill has a checklist, make one todo per item and work through them in order.
- When a Weakerpowers skill refers to the `Skill` tool, use Kimi Code's native `Skill` tool.
- Use Kimi Code's `Read`, `Write`, `Edit`, `Bash`, `Grep`, `Glob`, `FetchURL`, `WebSearch`, and MCP tools by their actual exposed names.
- When a skill asks to search file contents, use `Grep`; when it asks to find files by path or pattern, use `Glob`; when it asks to fetch a URL, use `FetchURL`; when it asks to search the web, use `WebSearch`.
- Weakerpowers does not use subagents, parallel agents, git worktrees, or automated tests. Do not dispatch `Agent` subagents or write test suites. Check work by looking at it and trying it the way a real person would.
- Save work by committing quietly under the hood and calling it "saving a snapshot" — the person doesn't need the word "git" unless they ask.

interface.shortDescription: A patient, jargon-free guide for building your first things with code
interface.longDescription: Weakerpowers walks absolute beginners through five friendly stages — brainstorm, plan, build, check, and save — turning a fuzzy idea into a finished, working thing one small step at a time. It makes the technical calls for you, explains things in plain words, and never writes code until you've seen a short plan and said yes.
